items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.q: Why must a recursive function have a base case?
items.correct: Without it, the function calls itself indefinitely, causing a stack overflow.
items.random_wrong: To make the function run faster.
items.why: Students treat the base case as optional ceremony. It's the TERMINATION CONDITION that stops recursion - each call adds a stack frame, and without termination the stack will overflow.

items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.
It's O(n log n). “O(n)” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Merge sort splits the array into how many parts?
items.why: Correct answer: 2. “log n” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Merge sort's recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n solves to which class? Choose the n log n equivalent in n=8: that is 8 * 3 =
items.why: The real figure is 24. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
